concept: A budget is not a restriction — it is a tool that makes sure your money goes where you actually want it to go.

hook.fact: Marcus earned €900 last month. At the end of the month he had €47 left and couldn't explain where the rest went.
hook.question: Sound familiar?

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: Most people do not run out of money because they earn too little. They run out because they do not track where money goes — and small, invisible spending accumulates into large, invisible losses.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: A **budget** is a plan for your money before you spend it. Not a record of what you already spent. Not a restriction. A decision made in advance about where your money will go.

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: **Intentional spending** means choosing where your money goes based on what actually matters to you. An unbudgeted student does not have more freedom — they have less, because money disappears into low-value spending before reaching high-value goals.

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: Budgeting does not require perfection or detailed spreadsheets. The minimum viable budget has three numbers: income this month, essential costs this month, and the gap left for everything else.

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: Now test this: Students who budget are not more restricted than those who do not. They are more likely to have money when it matters — a last-minute flight home, an unexpected textbook, a night out worth remembering.

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a budget?
options: To decide in advance where your money will go
To track what you already spent
questions.explanation: "A budget is a plan made before spending — it is about intentional choice, not restriction."

questions.statement: Budgeting is only useful for people who are struggling financially.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "Budgeting is a tool for intentional control of money regardless of income level. People on high incomes with no budget often overspend just as much as low-income earners without one."

questions.prompt: Match each statement to whether it reflects budgeted or unbudgeted behaviour:

pairs.left: "I check my balance before booking a concert
"I'm not sure why I'm always short before
"I set aside rent money on the first
"I'll figure it out if something unexpected comes

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "Choosing deliberately where your money goes based on your real priorities, rather than letting spending happen passively, is called ___."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: Complete: "Not being in ___ does not mean money is being used ___. A budget is useful at ___ income level because it ensures money reaches your ___ before it disappears."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Marcus's approach: "I check my balance before every purchase. If I have money, I buy it."
options: He checks his balance too often
A positive balance does not mean a purchase is affordable — he
questions.explanation: "A current balance includes money earmarked for rent, food, and bills. Spending based on current balance without accounting for upcoming costs is one of the most common budgeting errors."

tasks.scenario: Sofia has €800 income this month. Her fixed costs are €600. She has €200 for everything else.
tasks.prompt: She checks her balance: €800. Should she go?
tasks.explanation: "Balance ≠ available money. €800 − €600 fixed = €200 discretionary. A €150 concert leaves €50 for food, transport, and personal spending for the rest of the month. A budget would have made this trade-off visible before Sofia felt pressured to decide on the spot."

tasks.scenario: Build Marcus's minimum viable budget for this month:
tasks.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

inputFields.label: Remaining for food, personal, and savings

lockIn.takeaway: A budget is not a cage. It is the tool that makes sure your money reaches what you care about

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Intentional spending anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Intentional spending include beyond tuition?
